The Dutch had a yearly festival on December 6th to honor Saint Nicholas. They said "Saint Nikolas" quickly, which sounded like "sinterklaas," and when the English said that, it became "Santa Claus." Then it goes to Pennsylvania where German farmers called him Pelznickel, derived from "pelz" and "nickel." They had a tradition of a man dressed in fur bringing gifts on December 6th. Later, this evolved into the "Christkindl" and then Kris Kringle, which became another name for Santa. Thomas Nast, a painter from Bavaria, created the image of Santa we know today, inspired by his childhood memories in Germany.
